entries.description: A Philosophy of Software Design guidance for deep modules and information hiding. Use when cognitive load, shallow wrappers, leaky caller knowledge, tactical patches, or changes spreading across files make module or API design central. Clean Code owns readability; Code Complete, construction; Clean Architecture, policy direction; Refactoring, behavior-preserving change; Pragmatic Programmer, DRY, reversibility, feedback, and engineering practice. Co-invoke for independently central concerns.
entries.name: a-philosophy-of-software-design

entries.description: Clean Architecture guidance for inward dependencies and policy/detail separation. Use when policy independence is central and policy imports details, adapters own business rules, or core code constructs infrastructure. Layers, adapters, tests, and databases alone are insufficient. Clean Code covers readability; Refactoring, protected structural change; Legacy Code, blocked first feedback; Release It, production failure. Co-invoke only when policy dependence is also central.
entries.name: clean-architecture

entries.description: Clean Code guidance for locally readable, low-surprise implementation. Use when vague names, mixed responsibilities, boolean modes, hidden mutation, flow-narrating comments, schedule/rewrite pressure, or framework/concurrency detail obstructs a scoped change. Do not add it to an explicit refactoring-only audit or legacy-control task unless a separate local-readability decision is requested.
entries.name: clean-code

entries.description: Code Complete guidance for construction in implementation/change, review, debugging, refactoring, and tuning. Use when nontrivial work coordinates several concerns, or when solution-first coding, happy-path-only tests, mixed behavior/refactoring, guess-driven debugging drives code changes, or evidence-free tuning threatens defect control. It owns construction coordination; labels alone are insufficient, and another lens needs its own explicit objective rather than shared construction vocabulary.
entries.name: code-complete

entries.description: Designing Data-Intensive Applications guidance for distributed data correctness. Use when source-of-truth ownership, consistency/durability, replay/order, schema evolution, derived-data repair, replication/partitioning, or cross-system write/side-effect, transaction, or coordination semantics are central. A queue, cache, retry, database, or production failure alone is insufficient; Release It! owns operational survival and Clean Architecture policy direction unless data semantics are central.
entries.name: designing-data-intensive-applications

entries.description: Evans-style Domain-Driven Design guidance for discovering and evolving an implementation-driving domain model. Use when business complexity makes Ubiquitous Language conflicts, missing domain concepts, lifecycle rules, Bounded Context or Context Map decisions, Core Domain focus, or deeper-model pressure from awkward procedural behavior central. DDD Distilled owns the smallest-investment/selectivity gate; Implementing DDD owns post-boundary implementation mechanics.
entries.name: domain-driven-design

entries.description: Domain-Driven Design Distilled guidance for the smallest effective DDD investment and anti-ceremony selectivity. Use when domain complexity, language ambiguity, business differentiation, or integration risk makes subdomain investment, Bounded Context boundaries, Ubiquitous Language meaning, or whether tactical patterns earn their cost central. Domain-Driven Design owns deeper model discovery; Implementing Domain-Driven Design owns detailed mechanics after context and model commitment.
entries.name: domain-driven-design-distilled

entries.description: Implementing Domain-Driven Design guidance for implementation-level DDD after context and model commitment. Use when Aggregate boundaries, Value Objects/Entities, Repositories, Domain or Application Services, Domain Events, modules, translations, projections, or renamed-CRUD/anemic-model correction is central. Distilled owns the DDD investment gate; Domain-Driven Design owns strategic discovery and deeper model development; DDIA co-applies only for central distributed-data semantics.
entries.name: implementing-domain-driven-design

entries.description: Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture guidance for force-based pattern choice and responsibility placement. Use when choosing business-logic, persistence, transaction, concurrency, session-state, or remote-boundary patterns is central, especially Transaction Script vs Table Module vs Domain Model or Repository/Data Mapper/Gateway/Active Record. DDD owns model discovery; Clean Architecture dependency direction; DDIA distributed-data semantics; Code Complete construction.
entries.name: patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture

entries.description: Refactoring guidance for behavior-preserving structural change once observable behavior is known or protected. Use when renaming, extracting, moving, splitting, or another current code smell drives a behavior-preserving change. Dependency-seam work whose purpose is to gain first feedback belongs to Legacy Code until control exists.
entries.name: refactoring

entries.description: Refactoring.Guru guidance for code-smell classification and catalog treatment selection. Use when explicit smell classification, choosing between named refactoring techniques and their preconditions or tradeoffs, or a foreign-method-versus-local-extension decision is central after behavior is known or protected. Refactoring owns ordinary behavior-preserving structural change; Clean Code local readability; Legacy Code first feedback.
entries.name: refactoring-guru

entries.description: Use only when production failure behavior is an explicit objective or demonstrated operational problem, such as deciding failure semantics, demand/capacity limits, blast-radius isolation, degraded service, recovery, operability, deployment/startup controls, incident chains, or bounded resilience tests. Do not infer this lens from testability, safe-change work, routine rollout verification, local API design, ordinary errors, in-process concurrency, service/database calls, or money/transactions.
entries.name: release-it

entries.description: The Pragmatic Programmer guidance for accountable, adaptable delivery. Use when outcome ownership, duplicated knowledge, change fan-out, irreversible commitment, prototype fossilization, manual delivery/feedback loops, or blackboard-style coordination are central across a change. Clean Code owns local readability; Code Complete construction; Refactoring behavior-preserving change; Legacy Code blocked first feedback; Release It! production failure; A Philosophy of Software Design module depth.
entries.name: the-pragmatic-programmer

entries.description: Use only when a semantic change is blocked because current behavior is materially unknown, no trustworthy focused tests exist, or a dependency cannot be observed or substituted. The task must require gaining first feedback before changing behavior. Do not use when observable behavior and operational facts are already supplied, or when first feedback is not blocked.
entries.name: working-effectively-with-legacy-code
